title: Use One Top-Level GenieWorkspace Orchestrator
summary: A genie tooling project should load the workspace once, acquire managers once, call major system apply_ functions in sequence, and save outputs at the end.
when_to_use: planning a modular dat-editing project
explaining how to orchestrate many genie edits cleanly
key_points: Load the workspace once.
Call major systems from one thin entrypoint.
Save the dat file and registry at the end of orchestration.
pitfalls: Do not repeatedly load and save the workspace in many leaf modules.
Do not turn main.py into the whole implementation.

title: Split Genie Work By Gameplay Domain
summary: Use folders such as Classes, World, Inventory_System, Skill_Tree, and Character_General to keep data-editing systems isolated and composable.
when_to_use: explaining how to organize many data-editing features
deciding folder boundaries for genie mod code
key_points: Use folders as feature boundaries.
Keep each system independently includable.
Align folder names with gameplay concepts, not temporary implementation details.
pitfalls: Do not mix world, class, and inventory edits in one folder without a boundary.
Do not let generated artifacts define the main project structure.

title: Use Hierarchical apply_ Modules For Data Edits
summary: Large dat-editing projects should expose apply_ modules at the system level, with narrower leaf functions or modules handling concrete unit, graphic, sound, and component edits.
when_to_use: teaching modular dat-editing architecture
making systems callable in and out
key_points: Top-level apply_ modules should map to meaningful systems.
Leaf modules should create or edit specific assets.
A feature should be removable by dropping one high-level apply_ call.
pitfalls: Do not hide all edits inside one leaf module.
Do not let leaf modules orchestrate unrelated domains.

title: Use The Manager That Owns The Asset Type
summary: A real GenieWorkspace project should route edits through the manager that owns the asset type: units through unit_manager, graphics through graphic_manager, sounds through sound_manager, techs through tech_manager, and civ-scoped edits through civ_manager.
when_to_use: deciding which manager should implement a dat edit
explaining GenieWorkspace responsibilities
mapping a gameplay feature to unit, graphic, sound, or tech edits
key_points: Use the manager that owns the edited asset type.
Wrap cross-manager edits in one feature-level apply_ module.
Do not treat the workspace as one undifferentiated mutation surface.
query_terms: unit manager graphic manager sound manager
pitfalls: Do not hide cross-manager asset linking inside unrelated root orchestration code.
Do not blur manager ownership when explaining how a feature is built.

title: Centralize Shared IDs And Constants
summary: Shared IDs such as graphics, world objects, and stable constants should live in one config layer so multiple systems can reuse them without duplicating magic numbers.
when_to_use: explaining shared constants
avoiding duplicated graphic or unit IDs
designing global config layers
key_points: Use one shared config for truly global constants.
Import shared IDs instead of repeating raw numbers.
Keep project-wide IDs separate from leaf mutation logic.
pitfalls: Do not scatter shared IDs across many feature modules.
Do not confuse global constants with per-feature temporary values.

title: Keep Data Files Separate From Mutation Logic
summary: Use JSON or similar data files for mappings, item registries, and resource relationships while keeping code focused on mutation and linking behavior.
when_to_use: explaining data-driven dat tooling
showing how to separate balancing and mapping data from edit code
key_points: Store mappings and reusable data in JSON or config files.
Let mutation code consume those files instead of embedding large tables.
Use structured data to make retrieval and balancing easier.
pitfalls: Do not bury large mapping tables inside mutation functions.
Do not force retrieval to inspect code when a data file can hold the truth.

title: Copy Base Assets, Assign New IDs, Then Link Components
summary: Many genie edit flows work by copying a known base asset, assigning a new ID, changing selected fields, and then linking the result to graphics, sounds, or related objects.
when_to_use: explaining how to create new units or graphics from existing ones
teaching template-copy mutation workflows
key_points: Choose a stable base object.
Assign a new unique ID before broad mutation.
Link dependent graphics, sounds, or component objects explicitly.
pitfalls: Do not mutate the base template object in place.
Do not forget to update linked components after creating the new asset.

title: Link Units, Dead Units, Graphics, And Sounds Explicitly
summary: Complex genie features are often linked component graphs. Create the component assets in a controlled order and wire IDs explicitly instead of assuming the links will emerge automatically.
when_to_use: explaining linked asset creation
building a feature with main unit, dead unit, and graphics
teaching how to wire component IDs safely
key_points: Create component assets first, then wire IDs explicitly.
Treat dead-unit and graphic links as part of the feature design.
Keep linking logic in a dedicated helper or feature apply_ module.
pitfalls: Do not rely on implicit links when the project actually uses explicit ID assignment.
Do not spread component-linking logic across many unrelated files.

title: Save Outputs And Registries Explicitly
summary: A mature genie tooling workflow should save the modified dat file and any generated registry or audit outputs so the changes can be inspected and reused.
when_to_use: teaching reproducible dat-edit workflows
explaining auditability after edits
key_points: Save the modified dat file explicitly.
Save a registry or log when the tooling supports it.
Treat saved outputs as part of the workflow, not just side effects.
pitfalls: Do not rely on in-memory edits without a persistent audit artifact.
Do not leave output locations implicit when the workflow is meant to be repeatable.

title: Treat .dat Output Paths As Configuration
summary: The final .dat save location should be driven by project configuration or explicit user choice. Reusable guidance should not encode one developer PC path as the default answer.
when_to_use: deciding where to save the modified dat file
generalizing a local genie project into reusable guidance
teaching agents not to invent local save paths
key_points: Use configured paths when the project already defines them.
Ask the user where to write the modified .dat when no config exists.
Keep output-path policy separate from feature mutation logic.
pitfalls: Do not hardcode one local mod folder path as the general answer.
Do not hide output-path decisions inside unrelated feature modules.
